question: Define a closed set in a metric space (X, d) using the concept of limit points.
expectedPoints: A set F ⊆ X is closed if it contains all of its limit points.
A point x is a limit point of F if every open ball B(x, r) contains a point of F different from x.
Equivalently, F is closed if its complement, X\F, is open.

question: Prove that in any metric space, the finite union of closed sets is closed.
expectedPoints: Let F₁, F₂, ..., Fₙ be a finite collection of closed sets.
To show their union is closed, show its complement is open. The complement is ∩(X\Fᵢ), a finite intersection of open sets.
Since the finite intersection of open sets is open, the complement of the union is open, proving the union is closed.

question: Prove that a subset F of a metric space (X, d) is closed if and only if every convergent sequence of points in F has its limit in F.
expectedPoints: (⇒) Assume F is closed. Let (xₙ) be a sequence in F converging to some x ∈ X. If x is a point of the sequence, it's in F. If not, x is a limit point of F. Since F is closed and contains all its limit points, x ∈ F.

(⇐) Assume every convergent sequence in F has its limit in F. To show F is closed, show it contains all its limit points. Let x be a limit point of F. Then there exists a sequence (xₙ) in F\{x} converging to x. By assumption, the limit x must be in F.
Therefore, F contains all its limit points and is closed.
